lessons.title: Basics of Enum Types
lessons.summary: Learn how to define enumerations (enum), their key methods, and how to use them in switch statements.

contents.title: What is an Enum (Enumeration Type)?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Overview of the major new features in Java 17
- The meaning of LTS (Long-Term Support) versions
- Changes from Java 11 to Java 17

Java 17 is the mainstream version for enterprise development.

contents.title: Basic Enum Definition
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: What is LTS (Long-Term Support)?
contents.body:
| Version | Release Year | LTS |
|---|---|---|
| Java 8 | 2014 | ○ LTS |
| Java 11 | 2018 | ○ LTS |
| Java 17 | 2021 | ○ LTS |
| Java 21 | 2023 | ○ LTS |

LTS versions receive long-term support from enterprises, making them the most commonly used versions in production.
Non-LTS versions are released every six months but have shorter support periods.
contents.note: Oracle certification exams target Java SE 11 and Java SE 17 versions.

contents.title: Important Characteristics of Enums
contents.body:
1. Enums implicitly extend java.lang.Enum — they cannot extend other [[classes]]
2. Implicitly final — no subclass can extend an enum
3. Constructors are private only — cannot be instantiated with new from outside
4. Can be compared with == — == is preferred over equals() (null-safe and faster)
5. Automatically implements [[Comparable]] and [[Serializable]]
6. values() is a [[static method]] auto-generated by the compiler

contents.title: Using Enums in Switch Statements
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Summary
contents.body:
• Enums provide a type-safe mechanism for defining a set of constants
• name() — returns the constant name as a String
• ordinal() — returns the index based on definition order (starting from 0)
• values() — returns an [[array]] of all constants
• valueOf([[String]]) — converts a String to a constant (throws IllegalArgumentException on mismatch)
• In [[switch statement]] cases, write only the constant name without the type name
• Can be compared with == (recommended) — null-safe and fast

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: ordinal() returns the index based on definition order. RED=0, GREEN=1, BLUE=2, so the output is "2".

quizzes.question: Find the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: When specifying enum constants in a switch case, **prefixing with the type name (Day.) causes a compilation error**. The correct way is to write "case MON:".

choices.text: Day.MON is used in the case (type name is unnecessary; only MON is needed)
Enums cannot be used in switch statements

quizzes.question: What happens when Season.valueOf("spring") is executed? (SPRING is defined in the enum)
quizzes.explanation: valueOf() is **case-sensitive**. SPRING is defined in the enum, but "spring" (lowercase) does not match, so an IllegalArgumentException is thrown.

lessons.title: Advanced Enums — Fields, Methods, and Constructors
lessons.summary: Learn how to add fields and methods to enums and how to define abstract methods.

contents.title: Enums Are a Type of Class
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Writing multi-line strings concisely with [[Text Blocks]] (""")
- [[Type inference]] for local [[variables]] using the var keyword
- Defining immutable data [[classes]] concisely with [[Record classes]] ([[record]])

These features significantly reduce boilerplate code.

contents.title: Enum with Fields and Constructor
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Adding Methods and Fields to Enums
contents.body:
Java enums are not just simple constant enumerations — they can have fields, [[methods]], and [[constructors]].

- Each enum constant can hold data
- Constructors are private only (cannot be instantiated from outside with new)
- Abstract methods can be defined and implemented by each constant

Implementing the Strategy pattern using enums is a common practice in Java.

contents.title: Varying Behavior per Constant — Abstract Methods
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Enum Constructor Rules
contents.body:
1. Constructors are private only — public and protected cause a [[compilation]] error
2. When writing fields/methods after the constant list, a semicolon (;) is required after the last constant
3. If an abstract method is declared, all constants must [[override]] it
4. Enums can implement [[interfaces]]
5. Enums can define [[static methods]] and [[static fields]]

contents.title: Summary
contents.body:
• Fields, constructors, and methods can be added to enums
• Constructors are implicitly private (public/protected are not allowed)
• Abstract methods can be defined with individual implementations for each constant
• A semicolon (;) is required after the constants when members follow
• Implementing interfaces is possible, but extends is not
• Enums are ideal for implementing the Strategy pattern

quizzes.question: Find the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Enum constructors are **implicitly private**, and specifying public or protected causes a compilation error. The correct way is `Size(String label)` or `private Size(String label)`.

choices.text: The enum constructor is declared as public (only private is allowed)

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Since DIME(10), getValue() returns 10. name() returns the enum constant name itself as a string, so "NICKEL" is output.

quizzes.question: Fill in the keyword used to define an abstract method in an enum.
quizzes.explanation: In Java, the keyword to define an abstract method is **abstract**. When an abstract method is declared in an enum, all enum constants must override that method.

lessons.title: var — Local Variable Type Inference
lessons.summary: Learn how to use var for local variable type inference introduced in Java 10 and its restrictions.

contents.title: What is var?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[Switch expressions]] (switch that returns a value) syntax
- Arrow syntax (case X ->) to prevent fall-through
- Returning values with the yield keyword

An evolution of the [[switch statement]] for safer and more concise branching.

contents.title: Basic Usage of var
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Difference Between Switch Statements and Switch Expressions
contents.body:
Since Java 14, switch can be used not only as a "statement" but also as an "expression".

| Feature | Switch Statement | Switch Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Returns a value | × | ○ |
| break | Required | Not needed (arrow syntax) |
| Fall-through | Possible | Not possible (arrow syntax) |
| Exhaustiveness check | None | Yes (all cases must be covered) |

In a **switch expression**, unless all enum values are covered, a default is required or a [[compilation]] error occurs.

contents.title: Where var Cannot Be Used
contents.body:
**var** cannot be used in the following places (compilation error):

| Restricted Location | Example | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Field declaration | `var x = 10;` (at class level) | Local variables only |
| Method parameter | `void m(var x)` | Type is unknown |
| Return type | `var m() { ... }` | Type is unknown |
| Without initialization | `var x;` | No information to infer from |
| null assignment | `var x = null;` | Type cannot be determined |
| Array initializer | `var a = {1, 2, 3};` | Type is ambiguous |
| Lambda expression | `var f = (x) -> x + 1;` | Functional interface is unknown |

contents.title: Caveats of var — Combination with Diamond Operator
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Summary
contents.body:
• var can only be used for local [[variable]] declarations (not for fields, parameters, or return types)
• Initialization is required — the type is inferred from the right-hand side
• null / [[array]] initializer / direct [[lambda expression]] assignment is not allowed
• var is a reserved type name, not a reserved word
• Be careful with the Diamond operator <> — `new [[ArrayList]]<>()` is inferred as Object type
• From a readability perspective: recommended for use when the type is clear
• Java 11+: `(@Annotation var x)` is possible in lambda parameters

quizzes.question: Find the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: var can **only be used for local variable declarations**. Using it for a class field declaration causes a compilation error. The use of var inside the main method is correct.

choices.text: var is used in a field declaration (only allowed for local variables)
var cannot be used for String types
var cannot be used in the main method

quizzes.question: What type does the variable list become in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: When var is combined with the diamond operator <>, there is insufficient type parameter inference information, so it is inferred as **ArrayList<Object>**. You should explicitly specify the type like `new ArrayList<String>()`.

quizzes.question: Select all the situations where var **cannot** be used.
quizzes.explanation: var cannot be used for: (1) method parameter type declarations, (2) declarations without an initializer, (3) field declarations. var can be used in enhanced for statements like `for (var item : list)`.

choices.text: Local variable declaration without an initializer
Variable declaration in an enhanced for statement

lessons.title: Switch Expressions and Pattern Matching
lessons.summary: Learn switch expressions (arrow syntax, yield) from Java 14+ and pattern matching from Java 16+.

contents.title: From Switch Statements to Switch Expressions
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[Pattern matching]] [[instanceof]] (instanceof + [[variable]] declaration)
- The mechanism of [[sealed classes]] to restrict [[inheritance]]
- Listing permitted sub[[classes]] with the permits clause

New language features that further strengthen type safety.

contents.title: Switch Expressions with Arrow Syntax
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Three Types of Sealed Class Subclasses
contents.body:
Subclasses of a sealed class must specify one of the following three modifiers.

| Modifier | Meaning |
|---|---|
| final | No further inheritance is allowed |
| sealed | Allows further restricted inheritance |
| non-sealed | Removes restrictions, allowing free inheritance |

Sealed classes truly shine when combined with pattern matching.

contents.title: The yield Statement and Block Syntax
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Pattern Matching for instanceof (Java 16+)
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Summary
contents.body:
• [[Switch expression]] — can be used as an expression that returns a value (requires a semicolon at the end)
• Arrow syntax (->) — no fall-through, no break needed
• yield — returns a value inside a block syntax (not return)
• Comma-separated multiple cases — `case 1, 2, 3 ->` is possible
• Enum exhaustiveness — a **switch expression** must cover all enum constants or include a default
• **Pattern matching** for instanceof — `obj instanceof Type var` performs type check and variable binding simultaneously
• Pattern variables can be used with && but not with ||
• Flow scoping — pattern variables are available after `if (!(obj instanceof ...)) return;`

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: x=2 matches `case 1, 2`. Since arrow syntax is used, there is no fall-through, and "AB" is returned. The output is "AB".

quizzes.question: Find the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: To return a value inside a switch expression's block syntax, use **yield**. return means returning from a method, so it cannot be used inside a switch expression block. The correct code is `yield "Perfect";`.

choices.text: return is used inside a switch expression block (yield should be used)
A semicolon is not needed at the end of the switch expression

quizzes.question: Complete the syntax that performs type checking and variable binding simultaneously using pattern matching in Java 16+.
quizzes.explanation: With **pattern matching for instanceof** in Java 16+, writing `obj instanceof String s` performs a type check and simultaneously binds the variable s of type String.

lessons.title: Text Blocks and Records
lessons.summary: Learn about Text Blocks (multi-line strings) from Java 13+ and Record classes from Java 16+.

contents.title: Useful New Features in Java SE 17
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Improved [[NullPointerException]] messages (Java 14+)
- Addition of Stream.toList() (Java 16+)
- Other useful improvements

Small improvements that make everyday coding easier.

contents.title: Text Blocks — Multi-line Strings
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Indent Control in Text Blocks
contents.body:
The indentation of [[Text Blocks]] ("""...""") is controlled by the position of the closing """.

- Moving the closing """ to the left → indentation is retained
- Aligning the closing """ with the text → indentation is removed

Record classes can have a compact [[constructor]], which is a special constructor for validation.
contents.note: The equality comparison (equals/hashCode/toString) of a **record** is automatically generated.

contents.title: Record — Immutable Data Class
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Restrictions of Records
contents.body:
Records have several important restrictions:

| Restriction | Description |
|---|---|
| Cannot use extends | Implicitly extends java.lang.Record |
| Fields are implicitly final | Reassignment is not allowed (immutable) |
| Cannot add instance fields | No fields other than components |
| Cannot be abstract | Implicitly final |
| Can use implements | Interface implementation is OK |
| Static fields and methods can be added | No restrictions on static members |
| Methods can be added | Instance methods can be defined |
| Generics supported | `record Pair<A, B>(A first, B second) {}` |

contents.title: Summary
contents.body:
**Text Blocks**:
• Start and end with `"""` (a newline is required immediately after the opening)
• The position of the closing `"""` determines the common indent
• `\` suppresses line-end newlines, `\s` preserves spaces

[[Record]]:
• `record Name(type field, ...) {}` concisely defines an immutable class
• Auto-generated: constructor, accessors (no get prefix), toString, equals, hashCode
• Compact constructor — for validation (argument list can be omitted)
• extends is not allowed, implements is possible
• Fields are implicitly private final

quizzes.question: For `record User(String name, int age) {}`, what is the method name to access the name field?
quizzes.explanation: Record accessor methods have **the same name as the field** (`name()`, `age()`). The JavaBeans `get` prefix is not used.

quizzes.question: Find the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Records cannot have instance fields other than the components (fields defined in the declaration). However, **adding methods is allowed**. The `getGrade()` method itself is not an error.

choices.text: An instance field is added to the record

quizzes.question: How many lines does the content of variable s have in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The content of the text block is "ABC\nDEF\n". Since the closing `"""` is on a new line, a trailing newline is included. lines() does not include the trailing empty line, so lines().count() returns **2** ("ABC" and "DEF").

lessons.title: Sealed Classes and Java SE 17 Summary
lessons.summary: Learn about sealed classes finalized in Java 17, and perform a comprehensive review of SE 17 new features.

contents.title: What are Sealed Classes?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Features and APIs officially removed in Java 17
- Deprecation of Applet, SecurityManager, etc.
- Compatibility considerations when upgrading versions

Let's understand the status of deprecated legacy features.

contents.title: Basic Syntax of Sealed Classes
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Combining Sealed Classes and Pattern Matching
contents.body:
Using [[sealed classes]] makes [[pattern matching]] in [[switch expressions]] exhaustive.

- If all permitted sub[[classes]] are included in the cases, a default is not required
- When a new subclass is added, switch expressions without a corresponding case cause a [[compilation]] error
- This prevents missing handling for new types

Sealed classes are a powerful feature that enables "change-safe designs".

contents.title: Rules of Sealed Classes
contents.body:
| Rule | Description |
|---|---|
| Permitted classes are listed with permits | Can be omitted if in the same file |
| Subclasses must choose one of 3 modifiers | `final`, `sealed`, `non-sealed` |
| Subclasses must be in the same module or package | Inheritance from another package is not allowed |
| Interfaces can also be sealed | `sealed interface` + permits |
| Records are implicitly final | Records can be used in permits of sealed interfaces |

Meaning of subclass modifiers:
• `final` — completely prohibits further [[inheritance]]
• `sealed` — allows only further restricted subclasses
• `non-sealed` — allows free inheritance as usual (unseals)

contents.title: Combining Sealed Classes and Switch Expressions
contents.body: Let's look at the following code example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Java SE 17 New Features Summary
contents.body:
Major Java SE 17 features covered in this chapter:

| Feature | Introduced In | Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Enum (including advanced usage) | Java 5 | ★★★ |
| var (local variable type inference) | Java 10 | ★★★ |
| Text Blocks | Java 15 (finalized) | ★★☆ |
| Switch Expressions (arrow syntax, yield) | Java 14 (finalized) | ★★★ |
| Pattern Matching for instanceof | Java 16 (finalized) | ★★★ |
| Record | Java 16 (finalized) | ★★☆ |
| Sealed Classes | Java 17 (finalized) | ★★☆ |

• Exam tips: Frequently tested topics include special enum rules, **var** restrictions, **switch expression** yield vs return, and **record** accessor names (no get prefix)
• In practice: Robust data modeling using [[record]] + **sealed** classes is the mainstream approach in modern Java

quizzes.question: Which modifier cannot be specified for a subclass of a sealed class?
quizzes.explanation: A subclass of a sealed class **must specify one of** `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`. abstract alone is insufficient; it must be combined as sealed abstract or non-sealed abstract.

quizzes.question: Find the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: A subclass of a sealed class must always specify **final, sealed, or non-sealed**. `public class Cat extends Animal` without any of these modifiers is a compilation error.

choices.text: Cat does not have final/sealed/non-sealed specified
Cat is not listed in permits
A sealed class cannot be extended

quizzes.question: Select all the **correct statements** within the scope of Java SE 17.
quizzes.explanation: The correct ones are: (1) Record accessor methods do not have a get prefix (`name()`), (3) Subclasses of sealed classes must be defined in the same module/package. var cannot be used as a return type, and yield (not return) is used inside switch expression blocks.

choices.text: Record accessor methods do not have a get prefix
var can be used as a method return type
Subclasses of a sealed class must be defined in the same module or package
Values are returned with return inside a switch expression block
